grounding_note: Expansion batch: more amount/lien/posture disclosures in contractor and service disputes.

scenarios.opening_query: The company that waterproofed our basement did it so badly we now have standing water every rain.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || Real Property > Construction/Contractors
scenarios.hidden_fact: Mold remediation plus redoing the system is bid at $37,000, and the CCB complaint window closes soon.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The remediation and redo bids total $37,000 — and the CCB complaint window is closing.
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The fix is a $1,700 exterior drain correction and I'd rather do small claims myself.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's a $1,700 drain fix — I'll do small claims myself.
scenarios.vetting_note: CCB deadline adds the specialist hook beyond the dollar figure.

scenarios.opening_query: I need to take legal action against the flooring company that installed our hardwoods wrong.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Construction/Contractors || Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice
scenarios.hidden_fact: It's one buckled hallway — about $2,600 — and I want to handle court myself with a little coaching.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: One buckled hallway, about $2,600 — I'll handle court myself with some coaching.
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The whole main floor is cupping — $31,000 to replace — and their lien release paperwork was never given to us.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The whole main floor is cupping — $31,000 — and we never got lien releases.
scenarios.vetting_note: Standard reverse pattern; lien-release detail deepens the construction hook.

scenarios.opening_query: Our home builder won't fix the cracks that showed up in year one even though there's a warranty.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || Real Property > Construction/Contractors
scenarios.hidden_fact: An engineer traced the cracks to inadequate footings — a structural defect estimated at $85,000, and the builder's warranty company demands formal notice under the right statute.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: An engineer traced it to inadequate footings — $85,000 structural — and the warranty company wants formal statutory notice.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the defect is structural with statutory-notice requirements
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The engineer says it's cosmetic settling — about $1,200 of drywall and caulk I want reimbursed.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's cosmetic settling — $1,200 of drywall work I want reimbursed.
scenarios.vetting_note: Engineer's finding is the pivotal user-held document either way.

scenarios.opening_query: A tree service dropped a trunk section on our garage roof and their 'fix' made it worse.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || General Litigation > Property Damage
scenarios.hidden_fact: Their insurer denied liability claiming we 'authorized the risk' — the structural repair is $22,000 and we need the carrier fought, not a small case filed.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Their insurer denied liability saying we 'authorized the risk' — it's $22,000 structural, and the carrier's the fight.
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's $2,100 in shingles and gutter — small claims with a little guidance is all we need.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's $2,100 of shingles and gutter — small claims with guidance is fine.
scenarios.vetting_note: Insurer-posture disclosure moves the case past coaching.

scenarios.opening_query: We prepaid a solar company that installed half a system and then stopped answering the phone.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || Real Property > Construction/Contractors
scenarios.hidden_fact: We're out $52,000, the roof is open to weather where they stopped, and the electrician says the wiring they did is a fire risk.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: We're out $52,000, the roof's open where they stopped, and an electrician calls their wiring a fire risk.
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: We only lost the $1,000 site-assessment fee and just want that back through small claims.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: We're only out the $1,000 assessment fee — small claims will do.
scenarios.vetting_note: Abandonment-with-hazard is squarely the construction label; deposit-only version fits coaching.

scenarios.opening_query: The handyman who rebuilt our back steps overcharged us and the work is sloppy. We want to sue.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Construction/Contractors || Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice
scenarios.hidden_fact: The overcharge is $850 and the redo quote is $1,400 — every lawyer we called said small claims and offered nothing else.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's an $850 overcharge and $1,400 redo — everyone says small claims.
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The steps collapsed under my father-in-law — he's fine, but the code inspector condemned the porch, and the redo with engineering is $18,000.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The steps collapsed — inspector condemned the porch, and the engineered redo is $18,000.
scenarios.vetting_note: Same sloppy-steps story at coaching vs full-case scale.

scenarios.opening_query: Our kitchen remodeler wants his final payment but half the cabinets still don't close.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || Real Property > Construction/Contractors
scenarios.hidden_fact: He recorded a $23,000 lien on our house yesterday and says he'll foreclose it if we don't pay in 30 days.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: He recorded a $23,000 lien yesterday and threatens to foreclose it within 30 days.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a recorded lien with foreclosure threat exists
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: No lien — we're holding his last $2,000 and want to know how to defend a small claims case if he files.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: No lien — we're holding his last $2,000 and want to be ready if he files small claims.
scenarios.vetting_note: Recorded-lien foreclosure threat is the sharpest urgency fact in the family.

scenarios.opening_query: The pool company finished eight months late and the pool still leaks. We're beyond frustrated.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || Real Property > Construction/Contractors
scenarios.hidden_fact: The contract was $95,000, the leak is undermining the patio slab, and their lawyer already sent us a letter blaming our soil.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It was a $95,000 contract, the leak's undermining the patio, and their lawyer already blamed our soil in writing.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: contract size and whether opposing counsel is engaged
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The leak fix is a $900 skimmer seal and I just want my delay costs — about $1,500 — through small claims.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The fix is a $900 seal — I want about $1,500 of delay costs in small claims.
scenarios.vetting_note: Opposing-counsel involvement is a posture fact that ends the self-help option.
